title: Spinal Cord Compression
oneLiner: Emergency from malignant or non-malignant cord compression; rapid imaging and steroids/surgery needed to prevent irreversible paralysis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal epidural haematoma / abscess**
  - Single finding: **Fever + severe, localised back pain with rapid (<24 h) neurological deterioration** – even without signs of infection initially
  - Do not wait for MRI; give broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., IV flucloxacillin 2 g QDS + rifampicin [check local policy]) immediately if abscess suspected
- **Cord infarction (spinal stroke)**
  - Single finding: **Sudden (< minutes) complete flaccid paralysis with dissociated sensory loss** (pain/temp spared posterior columns) – no radicular pain, no cancer history
  - No time for steroids; urgent MRI + stroke pathway; consider thrombolysis only if posterior circulation stroke – very rare
- **Cauda equina syndrome (CES)**
  - Single finding: **Saddle anaesthesia + painless urinary retention** (absent bladder sensation on catheterisation)
  - Requires same-hour MRI and neurosurgery – misdiagnosis as simple mechanical back pain leads to permanent incontinence
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - **Back pain** – often nocturnal, band-like, progressive, worse lying flat (malignant)
  - **Leg weakness** – unilateral then bilateral, stiff or floppy depending on level
  - **Sensory symptoms** – numbness, tingling, band-like sensation at level
  - **Bladder/bowel dysfunction** – urgency, retention, incontinence (late sign)
  - **Gait ataxia** – especially if multiple levels or compressive myelopathy
- Key exam findings:
  - **Motor level:** weakness below a specific dermatome (e.g., L1 hip flexors, T4 intercostals)
  - **Sensory level:** sharp/dull and light touch demarcation
  - **Upper motor neuron signs:** hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski (below lesion) – but early or acute compression may be areflexic (spinal shock)
  - **Anal sphincter tone:** reduced or absent – do a digital rectal exam in every case
  - **Perianal sensation:** test all quadrants (S2-4)
  - **Postural hypotension** if high thoracic/cervical lesion
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Malignant spinal cord compression (MSCC)** – known cancer, subacute onset (days–weeks), nocturnal pain, sensory level, MRI shows extramedullary lesion ± bony destruction
- **Disc herniation / spinal stenosis** – acute, radicular pain, often motor only, no fever, no systemic symptoms; MRI differentiates
- **Spinal epidural abscess** – fever, severe localised pain, raised CRP, rapid progression; MRI shows inflammatory collection
- **Spinal haematoma** – on anticoagulation or trauma; sudden onset; MRI shows hyperacute blood
- **Transverse myelitis** – no back pain or mild, fever?; bilateral weakness with sensory level over hours–days; CSF shows inflammation; MRI may show cord oedema without compression
- **Guillain-Barré syndrome** – ascending weakness, areflexic from start, no sensory level, CSF elevated protein
- **Splitter:** **Absent rectal tone + saddle anaesthesia → CES (not MSCC)** – but always still image to confirm level
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full neurological exam documenting motor power (MRC scale), sensory level, reflexes, plantar responses
  - Rectal exam – anal tone and perianal sensation
  - Post-void bladder scan (if urinary retention suspected) or straight catheter
  - Vital signs (NEWS2), temperature
  - Assess for spinal tenderness / deformity
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U+E, CRP, coagulation screen, bone profile (Ca, ALP), LFT
  - ESR / myeloma screen if suspicion of myeloma
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - **MRI whole spine (T1 + STIR)** – gold standard; do as emergency (within hours, not days)
  - If MRI contraindicated (pacemaker, severe claustrophobia) → CT myelogram (contact radiology)
  - **X-ray spine** – may show vertebral collapse, lytic lesions, disc space loss; but NOT sufficient to rule out compression
- **Special tests:**
  - **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** if unknown primary suspected (but do not delay MRI)
  - **Lumbar puncture** – only if MRI excludes compression and transverse myelitis suspected; must check clotting parameters first
- **Never-miss test:** **Emergency MRI whole spine (within 1–2 hours of suspicion) – omission leads to permanent paralysis**
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Steroids:** Dexamethasone 16 mg IV/PO stat (unless contraindicated by high suspicion of lymphoma – but risk of paralysis outweighs; give after discussion with oncology if lymphoma known)
  - **Analgesia:** Morphine titration + paracetamol; avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment or bleeding risk
  - **Refer urgently:** Neurosurgery / oncology / spinal team (depending on cause)
  - **Catheterise** if urinary retention (do not wait)
  - **Log-roll precautions** if spinal instability suspected (avoid flexing/extending spine)
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Continue dexamethasone: 8 mg BD for 5–7 days, then taper (check local protocol)
  - VTE prophylaxis (if no bleeding risk): enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (or 20 mg if CrCl <30)
  - **Neuro observations:** motor power every 4 hours (document MRC level)
  - **Pressure area care:** turn every 2–4 hours with log-roll
  - **Bowel regimen:** laxatives (e.g., senna 15 mg PO BD + docusate 200 mg PO BD) to avoid constipation
  - **Bladder management:** trial without catheter after 48 h if no improvement; otherwise intermittent catheterisation QDS
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Definitive treatment: **radiotherapy (for MSCC)** or **surgical decompression** (for cauda equina, abscess, haematoma, unstable fracture)
  - **Rehabilitation:** physiotherapy, occupational therapy, spinal specialist nurse
  - **Pain management:** gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain, continue simple analgesia
  - **Antibiotics** (if abscess) – prolonged course (4–6 weeks IV then oral)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1 L/8 h if NBM for MRI/surgery; otherwise encourage oral
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (check bleeding risk e.g., recent spinal surgery)
  - **Diet:** normal unless NBM for procedure
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 Q4H, neuro observations Q4H (motor power, sensation)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - Dexamethasone: **16 mg stat (IV or PO)**, then 8 mg BD for 5–7 days, then taper (e.g., reduce by 2 mg every 3 days – **[check local policy]**)
  - Co-amoxiclav (for abscess): 1.2 g IV TDS if uncomplicated; adjust for renal function
  - PPI for steroid gastroprotection: omeprazole 20 mg PO OD
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Analgesia: morphine oral 5–10 mg PRN (or IV 2.5–5 mg PRN for severe pain)
  - Neuropathic pain: pregabalin 75 mg BD (titrate), gabapentin 300 mg TDS (titrate)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Dexamethasone: avoid live vaccines, monitor blood glucose (may need insulin)
  - NSAIDs: avoid if renal impairment, GI bleeding, or thrombocytopenia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce to 20 mg SC OD if CrCl <30 mL/min
  - Gabapentin: reduce dose if CrCl <60 (check BNF)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Dexamethasone: giving steroids without considering fungal/tuberculous infection** – if high suspicion of TB spine (e.g., chronic back pain, night sweats, weight loss), discuss with ID before steroids
  - **Delaying steroids for fear of lymphoma** – in adults >60 with no known cancer, risk of paralysis outweighs; give steroid and image urgently
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Complete paraplegia / quadriplegia within hours (if compression not relieved)
  - Neurogenic shock (hypotension, bradycardia) – especially cervical cord
  - Urinary retention → UTI, sepsis
  - VTE (DVT/PE) – risk high due to immobility
  - Falls (weakness)
- **Chronic:**
  - Spasticity, contractures, fixed deformities
  - Chronic pain (nociceptive + neuropathic)
  - Pressure sores
  - Bowel and bladder dysfunction (recurrent UTI, constipation, faecal impaction)
  - Psychological: depression, loss of independence
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** 30–50% at 1 year for MSCC (depends on primary tumour)
- **Recovery:** If patient can walk at presentation → >80% remain ambulatory after treatment; if unable to walk → <20% regain ambulation
- **Neurological recovery** best within first 48 hours; after 24 hours of complete paralysis, recovery is rare
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a rectal exam and perianal sensation test** – even if patient denies bladder issues; this is the single most sensitive sign of CES
- **Request MRI whole spine as an emergency** – even if only back pain with equivocal neurology; a normal MRI prevents permanent harm
- **Start dexamethasone immediately while waiting for MRI** – unless clear history of lymphoma with active treatment (then discuss with oncology first)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Not doing a full neurological exam** – especially lower limb reflexes and perianal area – leads to delayed diagnosis
- **Sending for CT spine alone** – CT misses extramedullary compression (e.g., epidural disease) and myelomalacia; MRI is essential
- **Giving steroids and then discharging without follow-up** – steroids can mask progression; patient must remain admitted until definitive management plan
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New leg weakness, loss of bladder/bowel control, falls, fever, severe uncontrolled back pain
- **Follow-up:** CNS/oncology/spinal clinic within 1–2 weeks; MRI if neurology changes
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / neurosurgery / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (or any single red parameter – e.g., RR > 24, systolic BP < 90)
  - GCS < 15 (new confusion or drowsiness)
  - Acute deterioration in motor power (e.g., from MRC 4/5 to 0/5 within 1 hour)
  - New saddle anaesthesia or painless retention (CES red flag)
  - Suspected spinal shock (hypotension + bradycardia) – call ICU
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Lactate > 2 (if sepsis suspected)
  - Urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours (neurogenic bladder can cause acute kidney injury)

rawMarkdown:
# Spinal Cord Compression

**One-liner:** Emergency from malignant or non-malignant cord compression; rapid imaging and steroids/surgery needed to prevent irreversible paralysis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal epidural haematoma / abscess**
  - Single finding: **Fever + severe, localised back pain with rapid (<24 h) neurological deterioration** – even without signs of infection initially
  - Do not wait for MRI; give broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., IV flucloxacillin 2 g QDS + rifampicin [check local policy]) immediately if abscess suspected
- **Cord infarction (spinal stroke)**
  - Single finding: **Sudden (< minutes) complete flaccid paralysis with dissociated sensory loss** (pain/temp spared posterior columns) – no radicular pain, no cancer history
  - No time for steroids; urgent MRI + stroke pathway; consider thrombolysis only if posterior circulation stroke – very rare
- **Cauda equina syndrome (CES)**
  - Single finding: **Saddle anaesthesia + painless urinary retention** (absent bladder sensation on catheterisation)
  - Requires same-hour MRI and neurosurgery – misdiagnosis as simple mechanical back pain leads to permanent incontinence

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - **Back pain** – often nocturnal, band-like, progressive, worse lying flat (malignant)
  - **Leg weakness** – unilateral then bilateral, stiff or floppy depending on level
  - **Sensory symptoms** – numbness, tingling, band-like sensation at level
  - **Bladder/bowel dysfunction** – urgency, retention, incontinence (late sign)
  - **Gait ataxia** – especially if multiple levels or compressive myelopathy
- Key exam findings:
  - **Motor level:** weakness below a specific dermatome (e.g., L1 hip flexors, T4 intercostals)
  - **Sensory level:** sharp/dull and light touch demarcation
  - **Upper motor neuron signs:** hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski (below lesion) – but early or acute compression may be areflexic (spinal shock)
  - **Anal sphincter tone:** reduced or absent – do a digital rectal exam in every case
  - **Perianal sensation:** test all quadrants (S2-4)
  - **Postural hypotension** if high thoracic/cervical lesion

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Malignant spinal cord compression (MSCC)** – known cancer, subacute onset (days–weeks), nocturnal pain, sensory level, MRI shows extramedullary lesion ± bony destruction
- **Disc herniation / spinal stenosis** – acute, radicular pain, often motor only, no fever, no systemic symptoms; MRI differentiates
- **Spinal epidural abscess** – fever, severe localised pain, raised CRP, rapid progression; MRI shows inflammatory collection
- **Spinal haematoma** – on anticoagulation or trauma; sudden onset; MRI shows hyperacute blood
- **Transverse myelitis** – no back pain or mild, fever?; bilateral weakness with sensory level over hours–days; CSF shows inflammation; MRI may show cord oedema without compression
- **Guillain-Barré syndrome** – ascending weakness, areflexic from start, no sensory level, CSF elevated protein
- **Splitter:** **Absent rectal tone + saddle anaesthesia → CES (not MSCC)** – but always still image to confirm level

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full neurological exam documenting motor power (MRC scale), sensory level, reflexes, plantar responses
  - Rectal exam – anal tone and perianal sensation
  - Post-void bladder scan (if urinary retention suspected) or straight catheter
  - Vital signs (NEWS2), temperature
  - Assess for spinal tenderness / deformity
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U+E, CRP, coagulation screen, bone profile (Ca, ALP), LFT
  - ESR / myeloma screen if suspicion of myeloma
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - **MRI whole spine (T1 + STIR)** – gold standard; do as emergency (within hours, not days)
  - If MRI contraindicated (pacemaker, severe claustrophobia) → CT myelogram (contact radiology)
  - **X-ray spine** – may show vertebral collapse, lytic lesions, disc space loss; but NOT sufficient to rule out compression
- **Special tests:**
  - **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** if unknown primary suspected (but do not delay MRI)
  - **Lumbar puncture** – only if MRI excludes compression and transverse myelitis suspected; must check clotting parameters first
- **Never-miss test:** **Emergency MRI whole spine (within 1–2 hours of suspicion) – omission leads to permanent paralysis**

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Steroids:** Dexamethasone 16 mg IV/PO stat (unless contraindicated by high suspicion of lymphoma – but risk of paralysis outweighs; give after discussion with oncology if lymphoma known)
  - **Analgesia:** Morphine titration + paracetamol; avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment or bleeding risk
  - **Refer urgently:** Neurosurgery / oncology / spinal team (depending on cause)
  - **Catheterise** if urinary retention (do not wait)
  - **Log-roll precautions** if spinal instability suspected (avoid flexing/extending spine)
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Continue dexamethasone: 8 mg BD for 5–7 days, then taper (check local protocol)
  - VTE prophylaxis (if no bleeding risk): enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (or 20 mg if CrCl <30)
  - **Neuro observations:** motor power every 4 hours (document MRC level)
  - **Pressure area care:** turn every 2–4 hours with log-roll
  - **Bowel regimen:** laxatives (e.g., senna 15 mg PO BD + docusate 200 mg PO BD) to avoid constipation
  - **Bladder management:** trial without catheter after 48 h if no improvement; otherwise intermittent catheterisation QDS
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Definitive treatment: **radiotherapy (for MSCC)** or **surgical decompression** (for cauda equina, abscess, haematoma, unstable fracture)
  - **Rehabilitation:** physiotherapy, occupational therapy, spinal specialist nurse
  - **Pain management:** gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain, continue simple analgesia
  - **Antibiotics** (if abscess) – prolonged course (4–6 weeks IV then oral)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1 L/8 h if NBM for MRI/surgery; otherwise encourage oral
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (check bleeding risk e.g., recent spinal surgery)
  - **Diet:** normal unless NBM for procedure
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 Q4H, neuro observations Q4H (motor power, sensation)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - Dexamethasone: **16 mg stat (IV or PO)**, then 8 mg BD for 5–7 days, then taper (e.g., reduce by 2 mg every 3 days – **[check local policy]**)
  - Co-amoxiclav (for abscess): 1.2 g IV TDS if uncomplicated; adjust for renal function
  - PPI for steroid gastroprotection: omeprazole 20 mg PO OD
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Analgesia: morphine oral 5–10 mg PRN (or IV 2.5–5 mg PRN for severe pain)
  - Neuropathic pain: pregabalin 75 mg BD (titrate), gabapentin 300 mg TDS (titrate)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Dexamethasone: avoid live vaccines, monitor blood glucose (may need insulin)
  - NSAIDs: avoid if renal impairment, GI bleeding, or thrombocytopenia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce to 20 mg SC OD if CrCl <30 mL/min
  - Gabapentin: reduce dose if CrCl <60 (check BNF)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Dexamethasone: giving steroids without considering fungal/tuberculous infection** – if high suspicion of TB spine (e.g., chronic back pain, night sweats, weight loss), discuss with ID before steroids
  - **Delaying steroids for fear of lymphoma** – in adults >60 with no known cancer, risk of paralysis outweighs; give steroid and image urgently

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Complete paraplegia / quadriplegia within hours (if compression not relieved)
  - Neurogenic shock (hypotension, bradycardia) – especially cervical cord
  - Urinary retention → UTI, sepsis
  - VTE (DVT/PE) – risk high due to immobility
  - Falls (weakness)
- **Chronic:**
  - Spasticity, contractures, fixed deformities
  - Chronic pain (nociceptive + neuropathic)
  - Pressure sores
  - Bowel and bladder dysfunction (recurrent UTI, constipation, faecal impaction)
  - Psychological: depression, loss of independence

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** 30–50% at 1 year for MSCC (depends on primary tumour)
- **Recovery:** If patient can walk at presentation → >80% remain ambulatory after treatment; if unable to walk → <20% regain ambulation
- **Neurological recovery** best within first 48 hours; after 24 hours of complete paralysis, recovery is rare

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a rectal exam and perianal sensation test** – even if patient denies bladder issues; this is the single most sensitive sign of CES
- **Request MRI whole spine as an emergency** – even if only back pain with equivocal neurology; a normal MRI prevents permanent harm
- **Start dexamethasone immediately while waiting for MRI** – unless clear history of lymphoma with active treatment (then discuss with oncology first)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Not doing a full neurological exam** – especially lower limb reflexes and perianal area – leads to delayed diagnosis
- **Sending for CT spine alone** – CT misses extramedullary compression (e.g., epidural disease) and myelomalacia; MRI is essential
- **Giving steroids and then discharging without follow-up** – steroids can mask progression; patient must remain admitted until definitive management plan

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New leg weakness, loss of bladder/bowel control, falls, fever, severe uncontrolled back pain
- **Follow-up:** CNS/oncology/spinal clinic within 1–2 weeks; MRI if neurology changes

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / neurosurgery / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (or any single red parameter – e.g., RR > 24, systolic BP < 90)
  - GCS < 15 (new confusion or drowsiness)
  - Acute deterioration in motor power (e.g., from MRC 4/5 to 0/5 within 1 hour)
  - New saddle anaesthesia or painless retention (CES red flag)
  - Suspected spinal shock (hypotension + bradycardia) – call ICU
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Lactate > 2 (if sepsis suspected)
  - Urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours (neurogenic bladder can cause acute kidney injury)
